data.title: Bigger Yard, Higher Fence?
data.description:
By Michael Arno, CFA

The interplay between policy-driven trade realignments, cyclical relative growth shifts, structural capital flow adjustments, and relative currency valuations could open new avenues for alpha generation in emerging markets.

Markets celebrated Trump’s November victory, anticipating a surge in mergers and acquisitions (M&A) activity, deregulation, lower taxes, and pro-business policies. However, in the first five months of the administration, we have seen a shake-up in global policy norms, severe equity market volatility following “Liberation Day” reminiscent of the early days of COVID, and an escalation of tariffs with China to an eye-popping 145%.

Despite a spike in uncertainty (see Exhibit 1) and falling polling numbers, the Trump administration continues to press forward with its efforts to reconfigure the global trade architecture. Its aim is to counter what it views as systemic imbalances, chiefly those stemming from China’s state-directed industrial overcapacity.

The administration’s strategy marks a shift from past multilateral trade frameworks toward a more assertive agenda, designed to level the competitive landscape and mitigate the distortions caused by non-market economies.

Over the coming months, will we learn if the small yard and high fence, a metaphor used by former National Security Advisor Jake Sullivan to characterize the need to protect critical competencies while still maintaining broad economic ties with China, still holds. Instead, a more aggressive trade rebalancing coupled with the cyclical and structural dynamics that are underway may expand opportunities in other emerging markets.

In alignment with this rebalancing strategy, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is zeroing in on the 14 most strategically significant US trading partners, seeking to foster a coordinated policy response to China’s industrial practices. By harmonizing standards and enforcement mechanisms across these relationships, the administration’s goal is to prevent regulatory arbitrage and increase pressure on Beijing to reduce excess capacity across a number of sectors, such as steel, aluminum, and solar.

If successful, this effort could mark a turning point in global trade dynamics - one that favors market-based production over state-backed overexpansion. A greater degree of decoupling in global trade dynamics may lead to a larger yard and higher fence than under the prior administration, potentially creating opportunities for other countries.

At the same time, financial markets are contending with the implications of a historically elevated US dollar real effective exchange rate (REER). Years of capital inflows into US assets spurred by higher real yields, fiscally supported economic outperformance, and geopolitical uncertainty around the globe pushed the dollar to extended levels.

While this US “exceptionalism” dynamic kept domestic financial conditions relatively loose, it also exacerbated America's negative net international investment position to nearly -64% of gross domestic product (GDP) (see Exhibit 2).

Cyclical, Structural, and Secular Tailwinds for EM

We see two potential drivers that could be a natural release valve for the dollar and a benefit for emerging markets (EM). On a cyclical basis, US growth is looking less exceptional as domestic fiscal stimulus recedes compared to the fiscal expansion expected to drive growth in other parts of the world.

On a structural basis, a rebalancing of global portfolios from investors overweight US assets after years of outperformance would lead to capital flowing back into undervalued or overlooked markets.

Additionally, EM may be well positioned to benefit from a secular shift in global production and capital flows. US policy aimed at curbing state-subsidized overcapacity may accelerate the relocation of supply chains toward other markets, including EM economies. This trend could catalyze investment opportunities in infrastructure, local manufacturing, and upstream commodities across Asia, Latin America, and Africa.

Latam: High Real Yields, Supply Chain Shifts, and Reforms

Latin America, in particular, stands out not only for its exposure to this realignment but also for offering some of the highest real yields globally, presenting a compelling value proposition for fixed income investors.

As we approach major election cycles in Chile, Peru, Colombia, and Brazil in 2025 and 2026, there is growing anticipation of a political pivot from the current wave of left-leaning administrations to more centrist, pro-market leadership. Such a shift could reinforce investor confidence, unlock reforms, and drive further capital inflows into the region, amplifying the economic benefits tied to global supply chain diversification.

Asia: Currency Opportunities and Risks

In Asia, investor opportunities could favor currency (FX) markets. However, many institutional investors in the region hold substantial US dollar assets, much of which remain unhedged. These holdings have created significant latent FX exposure that could result in rapid and potentially destabilizing currency adjustments if hedging behaviors change suddenly.

Taiwan recently illustrated this dynamic, where a shift in hedging flows triggered sharp appreciation in the local currency. US Treasury Secretary Bessent may have to ease off on requiring immediate currency adjustments as part of trade negotiations.

Otherwise, there is a risk of financial market headwinds. As rate differentials narrow and dollar strength fades, FX volatility in Asian markets may rise, creating tactical opportunities for investors with the flexibility to navigate these dislocations.

Central Europe: From Austerity to Growth

Central European markets, including Hungary, Poland, and Czechia, also present asymmetric upside as global investment flows begin to rotate. These economies are well positioned to benefit from a departure from Europe’s recent economic stagnation, especially if fiscal stimulus and targeted industrial policy gain traction.

Former European Central Bank (ECB) President Mario Draghi recently underscored this need in a widely noted speech, calling for a step-change in European investment, particularly in green energy, defense, and digital infrastructure.

As EU-level policy begins to shift from austerity toward activation, Central Europe may emerge as a key beneficiary of both domestic growth impulses and supply chain reorientation from Western Europe and Asia.

Conclusion: Starting with Trade, Several Forces May Reshape Opportunities

We could be witnessing a restructuring of global trade arrangements that have been in place for several decades, so these developments merit close monitoring. The interplay between policy-driven trade realignments, cyclical relative growth shifts, structural capital flow adjustments, and relative currency valuations could open new avenues for alpha generation while reshaping global risk premia.

As global trade norms are rewritten, taking advantage of these macro trends may offer both strategic diversification and enhanced return potential. During the current 90-day tariff pause with China, we will be focused on the progress of trade negotiations with other large trading partners, including South Korea, Japan, India, and the EU, looking for clues on how significant these changes could be and the implications for EM.

Index Definitions

The US Economic Policy Uncertainty Index reflects scaled frequency counts of articles in 10 leading newspapers. To construct the modern portion of the index (1985-present), monthly searches are conducted of each paper for terms related to economic policy uncertainty. In particular, the search looks for articles containing the term 'uncertainty' or 'uncertain', the terms 'economic' or 'economy' and one or more of the following terms: 'congress', 'legislation', 'white house', 'regulation', 'federal reserve', or 'deficit'.

The US Trade Policy Uncertainty Index is one of the category-specific Economic Policy Uncertainty (EPU) indexes developed in "Measuring Economic Policy Uncertainty" by Scott R. Baker, Nick Bloom, and Steven J. Davis. It reflects the frequency of articles in US newspapers that discuss policy-related economic uncertainty and also contain one or more references to trade policy.

The Consumer Confidence Index® is a survey, administered by The Conference Board, that reflects prevailing business conditions and likely developments for the months ahead. This monthly report details consumer attitudes, buying intentions, vacation plans, and consumer expectations for inflation, stock prices, and interest rates.

The Trade-Weighted US Dollar Index, also known as the Real Broad Dollar Index, was created by the Federal Reserve (Fed) to measure the value of the US dollar (USD) relative to the currencies of its trading partners. Weightings are determined by the share of trade with each country, with the five largest allocated to the euro, Canadian dollar, Chinese yuan, Japanese yen, and Mexican peso. These five currencies account for over two-thirds of the index.

JP Morgan Government Bond Index-Emerging Market Global Diversified Index - Latin America (GBI-EM Latin America) includes all of the local currency-denominated, regularly traded, liquid fixed rate, domestic currency government bonds issued in the Latin American countries represented in the JP Morgan Government Bond Index-Emerging Market Global Diversified Index.

data.title: UiPath: Missing The AI Boat
data.description:
While UiPath's (NYSE:PATH) share price remains depressed, it bounced higher after the company reported solid first quarter earnings. Revenue growth was stronger in the quarter but much of this was due to an easier comparable period and the accounting treatment of licensing revenue. ARR growth continues to decelerate, despite UiPath being well positioned to capitalize on the adoption of AI and agents. UiPath already has an expansive portfolio of products leveraging the capabilities of LLMs and designed to address AI and agent use cases, but these aren't expected to be growth drivers until next year.

I previously suggested that generative AI could be a threat to UiPath's business due to the technology enabling startups and potentially making it easier for organizations to develop their own automation solutions. While I continue to believe that this is the case, UiPath's valuation isn't overly demanding, and the company is transitioning to profitability. As a result, there is probably limited downside, unless UiPath begins to lose customers at a faster pace. A significant growth reacceleration on the back of agentic products will likely be needed to create real upside, though.

Market Conditions

UiPath reported soft results in the fourth quarter of FY25 and gave weak guidance for FY26. The company attributed most of this to the change in administration in the US impacting deal timing. UiPath believes that this will prove temporary and remains confident in its public sector business. Headwinds aren't completely isolated to UiPath's government business though, with the company's commentary also suggesting that weakness is building across the rest of the business.

UiPath appeared less negative on its first quarter earnings call, which was primarily related to early adoption of its newer products. The performance of the company's federal business wasn't discussed in detail, but it still appears to be a soft spot.

UiPath's poor fourth quarter results and soft guidance stand in contrast to most SaaS companies, and as a result, it is hard not to feel that there are company-specific issues. RBC also recently stated that checks indicate stable software demand, with potential upside to upcoming earnings and guidance.

UiPath Business Updates

UiPath offers a robotic process automation platform that leverages AI. In the past, the platform's capabilities have largely been based on fairly basic computer vision and natural language processing. Recent advances in generative AI are enabling more complex tasks to be automated though and UiPath is trying to capitalize on this with agents and orchestration. While this could help UiPath to automate the long tail of complex unstructured tasks that exist in most organizations, it is increasingly looking like UiPath is struggling to capitalize on this opportunity.

UiPath's strategic priorities include:

  • Introducing agentic AI products
  • Driving adoption within existing customers
  • Improving operational efficiency

New products in FY2025 included Autopilot, Agent Builder, Agentic Orchestration, Agentic Testing, and expanded GenAI connectors. Agent Builder helps enterprises build their own agents. It entered private preview in December, with hundreds of customers reportedly testing use cases, making it UiPath's most successful preview ever. Adoption has been particularly strong within financial services and healthcare.

UiPath's Agentic Orchestration product was also recently launched in public preview. This solution enables customers to orchestrate long-running processes across humans, robots, and agents.

Agentic Testing was also recently launched. It augments testers with AI agents to improve productivity. In addition, UiPath has introduced an AI-enabled software testing tool (UiPath Test Cloud). It includes features like Autopilot for Testers and Agent Builder.

Autopilot for Everyone is an area of strength at the moment, with the number of unique customers increasing 300% sequentially in the fourth quarter of FY25 and 60% in Q1 FY26. Autopilot for Everyone is a productivity tool that helps users automate work across applications.

While it is a positive that UiPath has managed to introduce a portfolio of solutions designed to capitalize on the opportunity created by LLMs, the appeal of these products and their impact on UiPath's financials is becoming questionable. For example, only around 20% of UiPath's customers have adopted AI products so far. UiPath is also still struggling to grow its customer base, and expansion within existing customers continues to weaken.

Agentic automation and Agentic orchestration capabilities are reportedly resonating with customers, but it is still early and UiPath doesn't expect these products to be material contributors in FY26.

UiPath is also starting to introduce vertical products, led by the recent acquisition of Peak AI. Peak AI's solution optimizes product inventory and pricing and UiPath plans on embedding these capabilities in its Agentic Automation Platform.

Financial Analysis

UiPath generated 424 million USD revenue in the fourth quarter, an increase of 5% YoY. ARR totaled 1.666 billion USD, up 14% YoY, with net new ARR strengthening sequentially but still down YoY. The transition to the cloud remains an area of strength, with cloud ARR increasing 50% YoY to 975 million USD (includes both hybrid and SaaS offerings). RPO was up 7% in the quarter, while current RPO increased 14%.

Q1 revenue totaled 357 million USD, up 6% YoY, while ARR increased 12% to 1.693 billion USD. Net new ARR in the quarter was extremely weak, though. RPO increased 12% YoY in Q1 and the current RPO was up 14%. While UiPath reported a large beat in the first quarter, the company had given more conservative guidance due to macro volatility.

UiPath expects 345-50 million USD revenue in the second quarter, which would represent roughly 10% YoY growth at the midpoint. ARR growth is expected to decelerate to approximately 11%. For the full year, UiPath expects roughly 9% revenue growth and 9% ARR growth. Growth of SaaS offerings is expected to present roughly a 2% revenue headwind in FY2026. As a result, UiPath's guidance isn't as soft as it may appear at first glance. Growth continues to decelerate, though, despite supposed AI tailwinds and UiPath's recent product innovation.

UiPath's customer count has been stagnant in recent quarters, with the company attributing the fourth quarter drop to customer hierarchy changes. Attrition is concentrated amongst smaller customers and UiPath's dollar-based gross retention rate has been steady in the high 90% range, indicating that churn is not a problem at the aggregate level. UiPath's dollar-based net retention rate declined to 108% in the first quarter, despite the company's expanded product portfolio and focus on driving adoption within larger customers.

The number of UiPath job openings has ticked higher in recent months, which isn't suggestive of a material deterioration in demand. There appears to be seasonality in this data though, which could account for the majority of the increase.

The number of job openings mentioning UiPath in the job requirements has been fairly stable over the past 18 months but remains at a depressed level. This is not surprising given UiPath's recent inability to expand its customer base and subsequent shift in focus to driving adoption within existing customers.

UiPath's non-GAAP operating income was 70 million USD in the first quarter at a 20% margin. Non-GAAP adjusted free cash flow was 117 million USD at roughly a 33% margin. Stock-based compensation remains an issue though, amounting to approximately 21% of revenue in the quarter. UiPath is trying to rectify this and expects 2-3% dilution in FY26.

Conclusion

While UiPath has a dominant position in a large market, its financial performance remains uninspiring. This was understandable in 2022 when macro headwinds began to hit most software companies hard. UiPath's business is yet to really stabilize though, despite over 2 years of soft growth, a resilient demand environment and emerging tailwinds from the proliferation of AI and agents. While UiPath could still be an AI beneficiary down the road, it is becoming harder to argue that this will be the case. Some of the company's current struggles appear to be driven by its exposure to government spending, though, which should rebound in coming quarters.

As a result, I tend to think that analyst expectations of high single digit revenue growth going forward are broadly correct. UiPath is also likely to continue delivering margin expansion through a combination of cost discipline and operating leverage. Assuming mid to high single digit revenue growth going forward and operating margins approaching 20-25%, it is not hard to see UiPath having a low double-digit PE ratio within the next 5 years. While this should mean that downside is fairly limited, UiPath probably needs a positive catalyst to drive the stock significantly higher.

data.title: Texas Roadhouse: The Resemblance To Costco Is Uncanny
data.description:
Texas Roadhouse (NASDAQ:TXRH) is becoming the ‘Costco’ of the restaurant industry, as it trades at elevated multiples in light of unparalleled resiliency and consistency of growth, with a seeming disregard to quarterly results.

The stock has now become a legendary outperformer and compounder, and I’m sad to say I’ve been on the side-lines through its ride.

Have we missed our chance? Let’s dive into recent results, and future opportunities to find out.

Introduction

I began covering Texas Roadhouse here on Seeking Alpha about two years ago, and I have to say, it is one of my worst misses as an analyst.

The stock is up almost 80% since my first article, more than double the S&P, and slightly less than double over the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ).

Looking back, there are two things that I clearly missed.

One, I failed to recognize the potential for multiple expansion, despite my view that this company is a top tier. I thought that it will eventually stay in its historical range of low to mid-twenties, whereas it now trades close to the thirties.

Two, I thought that same-store sales will have to come back down to pre-pandemic levels of low-single digits, but somehow (and I’m still not sure how), they are maintaining those high-single digit levels steady as a rock.

The combination of those two factors is what has been driving Texas Roadhouse to become one of the best investments of the past decade:

To my defense, I wasn't bearish, but rather on the fence, and I guess we can’t win them all.

Now, we can continue feeling sorry for ourselves, or we can try to look ahead and see if we should be chasing the stock here.

Texas Roadhouse Is Defying All Odds By Maintaining Its Post-Pandemic Growth Levels

In the first quarter of 2025, Texas Roadhouse revenues grew 9.6% to $1.45 billion, in line with consensus estimates. Growth was led by store openings, which grew 6.8%, and same-store sales growth of 3.5%, which included positive traffic.

Texas Roadhouse was a standout in the restaurant category with its positive same-store growth, though it still marked the slowest pace since 2016 if we exclude Covid.

Restaurant margins declined by about 80 bps from the prior year quarter, which benefitted from unusually low beef prices. However, they were roughly steady with last quarter, at 16.6%. Restaurant level margins for Texas Roadhouse should remain in the 16%-18% for the foreseeable future.

Lower in the P&L, operating expenses were generally stable as a percentage of sales, producing a 9.3% operating margin, and $134 million in operating profit for the quarter. Similar to restaurant margins, this is roughly the right range to think about going forward.

Looking ahead, Texas Roadhouse reiterated its guidance for positive comps, and 5% store week growth. Also, it increased its commodity inflation expectations for the year by 50 bps, including the potential impact from tariffs.

They also said that comps accelerated to 5% Y/Y in the first five weeks of the second quarter.

All in all, this was a business as usual quarter for Texas Roadhouse, which exceeded subdued expectations, after significant downward revisions heading into the print.

Footprint Expansion Is Slowing, And New Brands Have Yet To Ignite

Texas Roadhouse continues to view the 30 openings a year pace as its golden spot. As the company scales, these 30 openings are diminishing in impact, simply because 30 openings on a 600-store fleet are not the same as 30 openings on an 800-store one.

Still, management isn’t showing any sign of changing that pace, and frankly, why would they? This, again, reminds me a little bit of Costco (COST).

Us investors want acceleration and maximization of opportunities, but it seems like the recipe for success for these extraordinary-yet-humble companies is precisely the fact that they are not looking at things like we do.

The one thing that might be a bit worrisome, though, is Texas Roadhouse’s attempts at building other brands.

On one front, looking to build new brands might be a signal of saturation for the name brand, or at least a signal that saturation somewhere on the horizon. On another front, these brands are still not generating results that are anywhere close to the name brand, with AUVs that are 30% lower.

Looking around the industry, Texas Roadhouse is already at a location count that’s in line or close to the largest North American chains. This doesn’t necessarily mean there isn’t room to grow beyond that.

I just came back from a US road trip, and the only time I was close to a TXRH location was in Las Vegas. So clearly, there are places it doesn’t have presence. The question is whether those places are relevant, or is it a different crowd with different preferences.

Valuation & Prospects

At $196 a share, Texas Roadhouse trades at 29 times forward earnings, and 26 times its 2026 estimates.

These estimates reflect a continuation of the high-single-digit growth trend, along with steady margins, and consistent buybacks.

At this point, it’s hard for me to make the case for growth slowing down from the high-single-digit levels. Although, I’m not sure how they will be able to keep doing this with net openings decelerating meaningfully and comparable sales going back to earth.

I mean, shouldn’t restaurants be overpacked at some point?

Anyways, as long as Texas Roadhouse continues to grow at this pace, it is reasonable to believe it will maintain its multiple. Although it’s quite high relative to other dine-in restaurants like Darden Restaurants (DRI), the results and quality of the two is not really comparable at this point. The multiple is also quite high compared to chains like McDonald’s (MCD) and Domino’s (DPZ).

Texas Roadhouse’s growth rates and current valuation reflect a PEG that is among the highest in the industry at 3x. Again, I can't help but seeing a resemblance to Costco, which always trades at industry-leading valuations that somehow continue to go up.

Perhaps Texas Roadhouse is the Costco of the restaurant sector, and there are good reasons for it to be, but just like with Costco, I'd rather not be the last one buying at all-time high valuations.

Conclusion

For the risk of missing it once more, I still believe there are better risk-reward opportunities in the foodservice industry, especially with beaten down names amid the recent consumer spending concerns.

I believe that if and when the industry recovers, there will be names that will outperform Texas Roadhouse.

That said, Texas Roadhouse remains a best-in-class operator, and I wouldn’t run for the exits either. I guess that this setup deems another ‘Hold’ rating, and I encourage investors to look to capitalize on a future dip, should they occur.

data.title: Madison Large Cap Fund Q1 2025 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
The Madison Large Cap Fund (class I) decreased 0.93% in the first quarter of 2025, compared to the 4.27% decline in the S&P 500 index.

Portfolio Performance

The top five contributors for the quarter were Progressive, Fiserv (FI), Arch Capital (ACGL), Alcon (ALC), and Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A,BRK.B). Given the negative returns in the market during the quarter, it is no surprise to see three insurance companies top our list of contributors. Industry fundaments remain sound, but investors no doubt also found favor with their defensive demand profiles and limited tariff exposure. Progressive continues to benefit from robust premium growth and strong underwriting margins. While premium growth at both Arch Capital and Berkshire Hathaway has slowed a touch from the high levels of the last couple of years, it remains solid.

Fiserv and Alcon were also contributors in the quarter. Fiserv continues to do a nice job growing payment volumes, driven in large part by successfully taking market share with its Clover point of sale software terminal for small and medium sized businesses. Alcon benefitted from strong growth in its contact lens business which helped improve overall margins at the company.

The bottom five detractors were Alphabet (GOOG,GOOGL), Agilent (A), Accenture (ACN), Nike (NKE), and Brookfield Corporation (BN). Alphabet lagged largely on concerns regarding the impact of the U.S. Department of Justice lawsuit and its market position in artificial intelligence technology. We believe Alphabet is well positioned to absorb the potential negative effect from a regulatory perspective, as well as integrate new AI technologies in its search product.

Agilent’s shares got caught up in the broad sell-off in the life science tools industry as fears arose regarding retaliatory tariffs and budget cuts at the National Institutes of Health.

Accenture shares were weak in the quarter due to possible contract cancelations from the U.S. Federal government. While some lost business does seem likely, Accenture’s overall exposure to this end market is limited and the headwind appears manageable. Nike is still struggling as it attempts to clean up its over-inventoried position and revive new product launches. Given that its shoes are predominantly manufactured overseas, the recent round of tariffs will increase costs as well. Finally, while Brookfield Corporation reported strong quarterly results, its shares underperformed on fears of a slowdown in global economic growth.

Portfolio Activity

Overall portfolio activity picked up a bit in the first quarter.

We sold our investment in US Bancorp (USB), a large domestic regional bank. We have long admired its strong record of disciplined lending and its attractive mix of fee-based businesses. Together, these characteristics have led to a track record of generating best-in-class returns on equity. While these appealing characteristics are still present today, growth opportunities have waned as it has become one of the largest banks in the United States. We elected to sell our position and reallocate the funds to more promising opportunities.

We added to Arch Capital, Danaher (DHR), Texas Instruments (TXN), Elevance (ELV), and Ferguson (FERG) in the quarter.

Arch Capital shares were weak in February given concerns over slowing premium growth.

However, valuation looked compelling, and management has a terrific track record of prudently allocating capital to its highest and best use. Both Danaher and Texas Instrument shares dipped due to end market demand uncertainty in their respective end markets as well as U.S. trade policy.

Elevance’s profits have been pressured due to higher acuity in its Medicaid business. However, we believe the shares were attractively valued due to our expectation that margins should normalize as Elevance reprices its risk pools.

Finally, we added to Ferguson as its shares were weak because of concerns over deflationary pricing for some products and general demand trends in residential and non-residential real estate markets. In our view, valuation looked compelling given the solid long-term outlook for end market demand.

Respectfully,

Joe Maginot | Haruki Toyama | Rich Eisinger

data.title: Cenovus Energy: Disciplined Growth, Aggressive Buybacks, Real Value
data.description:
Introduction

With tariff uncertainty and declining oil prices, it has not been a great first half of the year for investors in Canadian oil. However, the oil industry is a cyclical industry. It can be a good idea to take a step back and look at the longer term.

Cenovus Energy (NYSE:CVE) has, like its Canadian peers, faced challenges over the past year, with the share price down over 34% in the past year. However, this integrated oil company has an improving operational base and clear long-term upside. With aggressive share buybacks, and a solid project pipeline, this recent selloff looks like a good entry point for investors seeking a long-term growth story.

With major assets like Christina Lake continuing to deliver, and several long-term growth projects set to come online in the coming year, Cenovus is set to see a boost in free cash flow generation. Combined with aggressive share buybacks, production per share is set to rise 40% from 2024 levels.

With management remaining disciplined regarding capital expenditure, I believe Cenovus is in a strong position. In this article, I want to explore Cenovus's latest earnings, its growth projects, and why I believe the current share price presents an attractive buying opportunity.

Q1 Earnings

Cenovus Energy recently released their results for the first quarter of 2025 with a strong operational performance.

Production averaged 818,900 barrels per day from upstream operations, a 2% increase from the same quarter last year. This near-record production was driven by strong performance at Foster Creek, which increased output by 7,500 barrels per day compared to the previous quarter, and small gains at the Sunrise and the Lloydminster assets. Whilst production fell slightly at Christina Lake, it still remained a top asset for Cenovus with production of 237,800 barrels per day.

Downstream throughput also saw a small rise from 655,200 in Q1 2024 to 665,400 in q1 2025. Canadian refining saw a record utilization of 104%, while U.S. refining ran at 90%, representing an overall utilization rate of 92%. Q2 is expected to be weaker with maintenance at the Toledo refinery, but this should bounce back in the latter half of the year.

With these near-record production levels and refinery utilization came a boost in revenues, which reached C$13.3 billion, up 17.7% from a year earlier. Net earnings, however, saw a decrease from a year earlier, reaching C$859 million against $1.176 billion in Q1 2024. This translated to net earnings per share of C$0.47 vs C$0.62 a year earlier.

This loss was driven by downstream US refinery operations reporting a fall in sales, and flipping from a C$373 million profit in 2024 to a C$455 million loss in 2025, with only the strong boost in upstream profits to C$1,974 on increased sales partially offsetting this loss. Overall free funds flow came in at C$983 million.

On the balance sheet side, there was a significant weakening. Whilst cash and cash equivalents rose C$368 million from q1 2024 to reach C$2.77 billion, long-term debt rose to C$7.52 billion with Cenovus now having net debt of C$5.08 billion, above the firm's net-debt target of C$4 billion.

This rise was in part due to changes in working capital and the C$595 million returned to shareholders in the quarter. This included C$62 million in share repurchases, C$333 million in dividends, and a C$200 million redemption of Series 5 preferred shares.

Whilst net debt remains above the C$4 billion target, management stated that the company was inclined to aggressively return capital via buybacks given current trading levels. Supporting this stance, the company has continued to aggressively repurchase an additional 10.9 million common shares for C$178 million through May 5.

Although Cenovus remains above its net debt target, I am supportive of buybacks at the current price. Too often commodity firms end up only repurchasing shares when the commodity price, earnings, and share price have reached a peak. By repurchasing shares while the commodity price and share price are lower, this gives greater shareholder value. Whilst this may be risky for some firms, given the low breakeven price of operations, this should not negatively impact Cenovus's balance sheet. In 2024 alone, Cenovus repurchased 2.6% of shares outstanding.

In addition, the annual dividend was raised 11% to C$0.80, with management confident the firm will remain free cash flow positive at $45 WTI. This is largely due to the nature of the firm's operations with over 75% of upstream production coming from oil sands. The extraction of oil sands is characterized by high upfront capital expenditures pre-production, but once up and running, decline rates are low, with fixed operating costs. Cenovus's Christina Lake boasts operating costs of only $9 per barrel, Foster Creek at $10 a barrel, and Sunrise at $18 a barrel, far below other methods of extraction.

Overall, Cenovus delivered a solid start to the year. The strong production and strong refining utilization points to improving results through 2025. While the oil price is weaker, Cenovus is taking advantage of the dip in its share price with meaningful capital being returned to shareholders. Combined with growth projects advancing on schedule, Cenovus looks well positioned for a bright future.

Growing Production, Growing Returns

Cenovus is approaching the end of its current five-year plan to expand production by 150,000 barrels per day, with several key projects nearing completion. These include both the traditional oil sands that make up the majority of Cenovus's production and offshore production, enabling Cenovus to boost both production and free cash flow growth.

At the Narrow Lake tie-back project, first oil is expected in Q3. Five well pads have now been drilled with steam introduced into the first two. This project is expected to add 20,000-30,000 barrels per day and connect to the existing Christina Lake facility leveraging its existing infrastructure and keeping costs low. Should this be successful, this will increase the potential for further expansion from the nearby Kirby West project, helping Cenovus grow production with lower initial capital costs, and taking advantage of Christina Lakes low operating costs.

In the offshore segment, West White Rose is nearing production off Atlantic Canada. Cenovus is preparing to tow the concrete gravity structure to the field. With construction now 90% complete, first oil is scheduled to be delivered by Q2 2026. Once fully online, production is set to be 45,000 barrels per day of high-quality brent with minimal incremental operating costs. Assuming an WTI price of $75, West White Rose is forecast to produce almost a C$1 billion in free cash flow in 2028.

Looking at the bigger picture, Cenovus is currently guiding for 2025 upstream production of 805,000 to 845,000 barrels per day, around a 3% growth from the previous year at the midpoint. As both the Narrow Lake tie-back project and West White Rose come online, production is set to reach 950,000 barrels per day by the end of 2027.

This production growth, combined with the share buyback program, is set to see Cenovus boost production per share by 40%, higher than any of its Canadian Peers. With the majority of capital investment spending expected to be complete in 2025, capital spending is set to fall sharply, boosting cash flow which can be put towards further shareholder returns.

With 8.5 billion barrels of reserves and a 29-year reserve life index, Cenovus is set for a bright future. As these low operating cost projects come online, production will increase whilst capital expenditures decrease, enabling Cenovus to enhance shareholder returns.

Valuation

Like most oil producers, Cenovus Energy's earnings are heavily dependent on the price of oil. Therefore, placing an exact value on Cenovus can be difficult. As such, I believe one way to value the company is in comparison to its Canadian peers such as Suncor Energy (SU), Imperial Oil (IMO), Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), and MEG Energy (OTCPK:MEGEF) (MEG:CA).

All its peers share similarities in being Canadian oil firms with significant oil sands operations. A simple comparison of earnings multiples will not suffice, given the vast differences in cash and debt levels. I therefore use enterprise value to EBITDA to compare, along with other metrics.

When compared to peers, we see that Cenovus Energy trades at an EV/EBITDA multiple of 4.57, a significant discount to the peer average of 5.43. This is also below the oil sector average of 6.27 despite the strong production growth profile of Cenovus and its lower operating costs due to the nature of the oil sands.

When we look at other metrics, Cenovus continues to perform strongly, with the lowest price to free cash flow multiple in the group at just 4.46. Whilst Cenovus's debt-to-equity ratio may be slightly above the peer group average, it is still within a healthy range and is partly due to the current focus on shareholder returns as opposed to debt reduction. Cenovus has the lowest return on equity cycle of the group, but this may be due to the current ongoing capital investments.

When we also consider beyond Cenovus's Canadian peers and compare to integrated oil majors such as Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), and Marathon Petroleum (MPC), the difference in valuation is striking. These companies have significantly higher EV/EBITDA and price-to-cash flow multiples, averaging 7.28 and 7.74 respectively, despite facing similar exposure to the oil price, higher operating costs compared to oil sands, and in some cases lower growth potential.

Even if Cenovus's valuation gap to US peers does not close, its lower multiples mean investors benefit from higher free cash flow yield and earnings yield. This means Cenovus has the potential to deliver stronger per-share returns over time through dividends and buybacks even if trading multiples remain unchanged.

While there are near-term headwinds from a lower oil price, Cenovus Energy shares look like a buy. With low operating costs, growing production, and continued shareholder returns, the company looks like it's in a strong position. When you combine this with the fact that Cenovus trades at a discount to not just its American, but also Canadian peers, Cenovus offers a compelling opportunity that looks set to outperform. For these reasons, I assign Cenovus a buy rating.

As with any investment, investing in Cenovus is not without its risk. The primary risk is, of course, the dependence of revenues and earnings on the price of oil. This is largely out of Cenovus's control, with OPEC decisions, global economic conditions, and shifts is demand all impacting the oil price. Weakness in the oil price will reduce free cash flow and potentially force Cenovus to reduce shareholder returns, though I anticipate Cenovus to remain free cash flow positive given the low operating costs of its operations unless the oil price falls significantly.

Other risks also include the impact of operational disruptions, whether from environmental conditions or oil spills, and the impact of new regulations, tax changes, and Canada's trade relationship with the US that may push up costs.

Conclusion

While Cenovus's refinery assets faced some weakness in the first quarter, Cenovus still delivered a strong set of upstream results and continued to return capital to shareholders. The annual dividend has been raised to C$0.80 per share and in the month following the end of the first quarter, Cenovus repurchased C$178 million worth of shares.

With major growth projects like Narrow Lake and West White Rose nearing completion, these low operating cost projects will boost production and free cash flow. This positions the company to continue increasing shareholder returns through dividends and aggressive buybacks.

With 40% projected production-per-share growth, strong reserves, and strong shareholder returns, Cenovus is a top performer among its Canadian peers. At the current share price, I believe Cenovus Energy is undervalued and assign a buy rating.

data.title: Q1 GDP Second Estimate: Real GDP At -0.2%, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The U.S. economy contracted for the first time in three years to start off 2025. The BEA's second estimate of real gross domestic product showed economic growth decreased at an annual rate of 0.2% in Q1 2025. The latest estimate was higher than the -0.3% forecast and lower than the Q4 final estimate of 2.5%.

Here is the opening text from the Bureau of Economic Analysis news release:

Real gross domestic product (GDP) decreased at an annual rate of 0.2 percent in the first quarter of 2025 (January, February, and March), according to the second estimate released by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. In the fourth quarter of 2024, real GDP increased 2.4 percent. The decrease in real GDP in the first quarter primarily reflected an increase in imports, which are a subtraction in the calculation of GDP, and a decrease in government spending. These movements were partly offset by increases in investment, consumer spending, and exports..

Gross Domestic Product (GDP)

Real gross domestic product (GDP) measures how fast or slowly the economy is growing and measures the inflation-adjusted value of all goods and services produced by the economy. It is considered the broadest measure of economic activity and the primary indicator of an economy's health.

The Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) releases real GDP data on a monthly basis. There are 3 versions released a month apart, advance, second, and final, each incorporating data that was previously unavailable. Economists can use GDP to determine whether an economy is growing or experiencing a recession.

This chart presents the annualized percentage change in real (inflation-adjusted) gross domestic product from the preceding quarter, beginning in Q2 1947. Prior to that, GDP was calculated annually. To provide further context, the chart includes recessions as determined by the National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER), the historical average of 3.20%, and the current 10-year moving average of 2.61%.

As the data clearly shows, the 10-year moving average fell below the historical average in 2007 and has remained there since, illustrating a significant slowdown in U.S. economic growth following the Great Recession. We are currently at a level below both the 10-year moving average and the series average, indicating the current economic growth is on the historically slower end.

The chart above has many interesting data points but can be quite overwhelming to look at. This next chart provides a simplified look over the past few years.

Here is a log-scale chart of real GDP with an exponential regression, which helps us understand growth cycles since the 1947 inception of quarterly GDP. The regression illustrates the fact that the current trend, since the Great Recession, has a visibly lower slope than the long-term trend. In fact, the current GDP is 13.0% below the pre-recession trend (2008).

A particularly telling representation of slowing growth in the US economy is the year-over-year rate of change. Beginning in 1947, the average year-over-year rate at the start of recessions is 3.2% with a range of 1.28% to 6.78%. The current year-over-year rate for real GDP is at 2.06%, a level at or below 9 of the 12 recession starts during this time frame.

GDP: A Look Ahead

In the chart below, we use the Conference Board's Economic Forecast for the U.S. Economy to visualize GDP forecast over the next few years.

May 15, 2025

Tariff Inflation to Temper Growth Despite Trade Deal Cheers

We estimate sizable shocks to growth, inflation, and employment in the coming months, even as the U.S. administration reached an agreement to significantly reduce tariffs on imports from China. The Conference Board estimates tariffs may substantially lower GDP growth , raise inflation, weaken the labor market, and prompt Fed rate cuts. The latest developments also suggest the Fed may cut rates only twice this year.

Original Post

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: The Fog Of Tariffs
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Nvidia gets another shrug from Wall Street post-earnings (0:25). Valuing stocks has become more difficult (4:05). Salesforce's interesting earnings (7:30). Off the radar Deckers Outdoors' volatility (9:00). Tariff uncertainty continues (11:15). Longer-term economic conversation (15:40). Musk, DOGE, and Tesla (17:00). Murky retail picture (18:25). Companies investing in Bitcoin (23:25).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, our Director of news at Seeking Alpha, welcome back to Wall Street Roundup.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here.

RS: Always great to talk to you and see how things are going in the markets and in the world and in the economy. I think probably the big stock name to report this week that we discussed last week in anticipation of the earnings release is everybody's favorite AI stock, NVIDIA (NVDA). What would you say about what you saw out of NVIDIA and the market's reaction to it?

BS: I feel that NVIDIA is fomenting a real debate among the investor community. So far today, the stock is up about 4% as we're talking now on Thursday. It was up more earlier in the day, but it's pulled back a little bit.

So a modestly positive response to the earnings. To put that into perspective, the stock's trading at about $140 now. Its fifty two week high was $153, just over $153. So it's about 9% away from that fifty-two week high.

Looking the other way from April lows, the stock is up 62%, so it's already had a giant rebound from the nadir of the tariff concerns back in April. So the company's earnings this time around is basically receiving a shrug from Wall Street.

If you ask the Bulls, they'll tell you that the company is seeing macro problems with its China sales, but outside of that headwind, there's strength in other geographies and strength in areas like gaming, so even outside the AI sector.

However, on the other side, the bears would say that the AI related data center growth has been slowing the last several quarters. So it reached a peak in Q1 of last year at 427% annual growth.

It's been less and less each quarter since, until this last quarter, 73%. Year-over-year growth, obviously, that's still pretty strong, especially when you're lapping a 400% gain last year.

The data center revenue was now 10 times what it was in Q4 of 2023. So it's currently Q1 of '26 the way that they do their calendar quarter they do their fiscal quarter. So little over two years, you've seen NVIDIA increase their data center revenue by 10 times.

So the question now just becomes where are we in that growth cycle? Have we captured all the growth there is to capture and now it's just gonna level off? Or is there another wave coming?

RS: And what are the data points to pay attention to along the way until their next release, would you say?

BS: I think looking at other players in the sector, Broadcom (AVGO), for instance, is reporting its earnings next week. Obviously, that's probably a little soon to give us any new information that we didn't already hear from NVIDIA.

But tracking those kinds of results and especially the management commentary, I think there's a lot of headline risk, both up and down, for all these stocks just based on the international situation, tariffs and import curbs and other issues that make it difficult to predict exactly how sales are going to play out in different geographies.

So I would keep an eye on those headlines as well.

And then look for spending predictions from other companies. Meta (META), in its last earnings report, rather high profile, discussed how it's still building out the spending for its data centers.

So if you're seeing other major players like that discuss their ongoing spending, that's a pretty good sign for companies like NVIDIA.

RS: And talking about valuing these stocks is something that we've talked about on Investing Experts on that podcast a little bit in terms of a Meta, like an Nvidia, like a Broadcom, like a Tesla (TSLA), like a Palantir (PLTR), where the news is promising but not definite, how would you say, or what do you see out of the investing community that is attempting to navigate that?

Or what are some points that you would highlight in terms of investors trying their best to sufficiently value these stocks?

BS: I think you hit the nail on the head in terms of that being the central debate about the valuation of these large companies.

And I think from an investor's point of view, it points to a situation where you can be right on the theme but wrong on the particular player. I think it speaks to being diversified within the AI space. If you think that that's a major growth area, there's still room to run there.

One example I have for the past week is Intuit (INTU), which is the company that runs TurboTax. They rose 8% on Friday after reporting their earnings and then another 4% Tuesday, which is the first day of the trading week this week.

So that pushed it to a 52 high. It's up 42% from its April lows. It beat guidance, it beat expectations and raised guidance, thanks in large part to a strong showing for its TurboTax Live product.

But in the wake of that, analysts were also pointing to the benefits that are working through the system from the AI integration of products like Mailchimp and QuickBooks and their other products that they provide that help people run their small businesses.

So you're seeing the AI build out starting to drop to the bottom line to some of these companies. So the bet might not completely be in the infrastructure place going forward.

I think that as an investor, if you're bullish on AI, which I think most people are, it's about finding the companies that have the most leverage in that area and finding ones that haven't popped yet.

RS: Is anyone bearish on AI at this point? Like, to your point about most people are bullish on AI. Do you hear any bearish take still?

BS: No. I mean, you have doomers who would say something like, well, you know, eventually AI is going to launch the missiles and destroy us all. Those kinds of arguments.

Another sort of bearish argument would be it's overhyped that the promise of AI now isn't going to play out, at least not play out in as near a term as we had hoped. But I don't know. I think anybody who's even played with ChatGPT in any meaningful way can see how impressive it is in terms of productivity tool.

And as a company, it's just a matter of figuring out what AI does well, what it doesn't quite do well yet, and then putting in a position to help you do whatever business you're running.

So I think, again, it's just kind of an execution risk would be the bearish look. And I think that really kinda goes company to company, whether a company has a near term opportunity to leverage it or whether it's a longer-term play.

RS: So sticking with the earnings theme, we saw some other names report this week, Salesforce (CRM) being one of the bigger names. What did you see out of Salesforce, and what other earnings were you paying attention to this week?

BS: Yeah. Salesforce is an interesting one, especially on the topic of near term versus longer-term impacts of AI. Salesforce is a company that's been benefiting from putting AI into a lot of its, customer-facing products.

They just announced an $8,000,000,000 deal to buy the cloud data management company Informatica. There's some worry about that integration, just how quickly it's going to happen, whether integrating that is going to be distraction for the AI projects that are already underway at the company, whether that's going to delay some of the rollouts while management is distracted somewhat.

So the stock is down 6% Thursday following its earnings reports. There was nothing terrible in the earnings, but a lot of the benefits, the better than expected results, were helped by foreign exchange. So there's a sign that maybe the underlying fundamentals were not as strong as many investors would hope.

In terms of execution risk, I think that's a company that's a good example of a place where there are some concerns about whether or not the hope for the AI rollout is going to be delayed more than people had expected.

RS: A name that reported last week, but that saw some reverberations from the report this week, was Deckers Outdoors (DECK). And I think that might be a nice stock to discuss as a segue into the tariff conversation. There was a big decline and then there was a bit of a rebound. What would you say about the news coming out of Deckers?

BS: A company that's off the radar a little bit, I think, for most investors, but I do think that it represents a good test case for the tariff situation.

I would say the two headlines that have dominated the market so far this week have been NVIDIA earnings and then the unfolding tariff drama that continues, and Deckers Outdoor is a good example of that.

It was down 19% last Friday following the release of its earnings. There was nothing major for the last quarter that was particularly disturbing, but the company gave guidance with a large hit from tariffs.

So it's a company where there was a huge worry about how the tariffs are going to affect the bottom line. But then over the weekend when Trump announced the moratorium on the EU tariffs that allowed the company to the stock to bounce back significantly.

It went down 19% and then bounced back more than 7% in the following trading session on that new. So it's just an example of retailers especially, but any company that has supply chains tied to international trade is in danger of being whipsawed as the headlines bounce around.

RS: In terms of the tariff talk, I was talking to a friend that lives outside of the US, and it's interesting to hear people's opinions outside of the US and what kind of news they're getting. And it seems that there is a sense that the tariffs, once there is a decision made, might bring some stability to the markets.

But my point was we're still in a wait and see game with the tariffs, and then, boom, this news comes out, and it seems even more apparent that there is nothing settled in the tariff conversation. What are you seeing out of that news? How the market's reacted? How investors are reacting and your thoughts?

BS: I agree with the notion that certainty at whatever level of tariff is better than the uncertainty that's happening now, just in terms of the market setting some sort of floor and moving forward.

I mean, if companies can make longer-term decisions based on whatever the environment's going to be, then things can start to settle into a normal pattern.

I think the risk there is Trump's strategy seems to perpetually negotiate, to continue to delay and then announce and then put pressure on it which could very well work out to the benefit in the long term as long as the deal is eventually finally struck.

Meanwhile, you're dealing with each of these geographies separately, China, India, the EU. So there's not really room for kind of grand bargain to be struck. So it's all gonna be happening in dribs and drabs as the negotiations move forward.

To your point, I think that complicates the situation. I don't see sort of a moment of clarity happening. I don't see coming out of the fog in any near term. I think it's just something investors are going to have to be used to at least I'd say over the next ninety days, maybe through the rest of 2025, maybe beyond that, who knows?

I think investors are going to have to get used to operating in that sense of not knowing quite when a shoe might drop in terms of tariffs.

RS: Being comfortable with being uncomfortable definitely seems to be the era that we're in. Did you see any of this coming? Was there a sense of something like this coming down the road? Was it in the news that this might be happening?

BS: I certainly didn't have an inkling of this particular news. I think I've gotten used to the idea that anything that's happened is reversible.

And I should mention too, it's not just Trump administration policy and negotiating tactics. There's also the court's decision recently to stop the April tariffs and the other players in the game.

There's other countries taking steps in response to that as well. So you have many, many different players. It's not just a Trump watching situation.

It becomes much more complicated than that. So like I said, I don't know if you're more plugged into the situation in various markets, you might be able to predict whether something's gonna happen. I would advise investors to not try and get ahead of headlines, but create a portfolio that has sufficient shock absorbers that it's not going to be taken terribly by surprise when things go in an unexpected direction.

RS: And I think the very nature of the tariff conversation is its unpredictability. I mean, there is no way that anybody has a sense of what's gonna be happening from day to day or from week to week or from month to month. It's just impossible.

BS: Right. And just the market movements in response are so significant. You know, you'll see two, three, four percent moves, depending on the headline on that given day.

I'm talking Nasdaq or the S&P 500 (SP500), and much stronger in individual stocks depending on how leveraged they are to that situation. So it becomes, a matter of, as an investor, how much of that uncertainty can you absorb or near term excitement because the markets also swing the other way.

And other investors, ones that don't have that appetite for the swings, might be looking for safer places in the market to stash money until things clarify a little bit, looking for defensive areas that might be a little less susceptible to day-to-day movement.

RS: And how are you seeing this affect the macro data points, the macro conversation, how we should be thinking about the US broadly speaking and internationally for that matter? What are you seeing out of the broad macroeconomic picture?

BS: It's interesting because I feel that the underlying economic conversation has kind of gone by the wayside.

People are either talking about tariffs and administrative policy as it affects the markets, or talking about individual companies like NVIDIA or Salesforce. But meanwhile, there's a there's kind of a longer-term economic conversation going on.

Meanwhile, there's still debate about what the Fed should be doing, whether inflation has been tamed enough to start cutting rates or whether they're still going to be in a wait and see situation. We have jobs data coming out next week, which is going to be a big signpost in terms of what the near term economy is doing.

So kind of under the surface or off the radar, however you wanna frame that metaphor, you have the overall economy just kind of churning somewhat uncertainly. So that's another looming risk that could start affecting markets if the news started to get worse.

RS: And anything to say vis a vis Tesla in terms of Musk getting out of DOGE?

We've been talking about it so much on the podcast - we had Gary Vaughan on Investing Experts talking about how he hopes that Musk gets out of politics to save the company. There's been a lot of that sentiment shared, so I would assume it's mostly a positive reaction to this news. But do you have anything worthwhile to say about that?

There are lots of unworthwhile things to say, I know.

BS: Of course. Any Tesla investor, like a philosophical precept, would say that having your CEO full time at the company is better than not having your CEO at the company.

I can't imagine that anyone thinks him coming back is bearish unless you have a negative opinion of Elon Musk in general, in which case maybe your argument is, you know, better that he gets distracted.

The real debate going on is to what extent has the brand been tarnished in particular areas. I'm thinking especially in Europe where sales are down dramatically since he went off on his DOGE quest.

Whether or not those sales can bounce back, whether or not things can return to normal for Tesla, or whether this side quest has damaged or altered at least the situation for Tesla in a way that's going to take a while to play out.

RS: I wanted to touch base about the retail stocks we talked about last week in terms of Target (TGT) and Gap (GAP) and what we were seeing out of the retail space we discussed a bit last week. Anything to update listeners with there?

BS: I think the general retail sector is very hard to read right now. So many of them are tied into the tariff situation that really those headlines are going to play a big part.

I haven't checked in the last couple of hours, but as of the open this morning, the impact of the court decision about tariffs has been pretty muted in the retail sector.

So I think there's a sense in which investors in that sector are becoming immune to individual headlines. So I think that that points to a stock picker's market within retailers. If you're interested in that area, you wanna seek out the companies that have some protections against tariff risk.

As we talked about earlier, Deckers Outdoors is a great example of a company that can swing dramatically based on the outlook for tariffs.

So as you and I have talked about, a few times, a company like Walmart (WMT) that has some negotiating leverage within its suppliers, could be protected somewhat from that. They just have more options than a smaller company with less resources. I would look towards those.

A data point that's coming out in the near future would be Costco's (COST) earnings. Costco has been taking market share from companies like Target. So like Walmart, it's been a solid performer in an uncertain market.

So I think seeing those results will be an interesting signpost for the retail sector.

RS: And is the sense about Costco that they've taken that market share because of the protests around Target, or is it more than that?

BS: So I have to admit, I'm a little suspicious that the protests around Target are enough to damage it as dramatically as has happened. I think there might be several factors at the same time that could be having a near term effect, plus macro factors that are eating into sales.

But certainly, Target has been among if you count Walmart, Costco, and Target as kind of the trio at the top of the brick and mortar retail environment, Target's been the loser among those three.

I think the reasons are complex, but it'll be interesting to see to what extent Target can plant its feet and start to make the turnaround happen.

RS: What would you say are the main reasons for the market share loss? Is it Costco hot dogs and pizza? Is it more than that?

BS: Yeah. Probably. I think Target has an interesting place in the retailing world in that it's lower costs obviously than a luxury brand or something like that. But compared to its direct competitors, the Costcos and Walmarts of the world, it's a step-up in terms of price points.

And I think that it was uniquely ill prepared for the coming political and macro storms that were coming. I just don't think it was very well positioned for the particular macro situation that has emerged in, say, the past year. It's a matter of how quickly can Target reorient itself.

And it's also a question of whether or not it requires a broad rethinking of its position in the retailing world or whether it's just a matter of recapturing the audience, the customer base that it's seen seep away to places like Walmart and Costco.

RS: So earnings coming up next week, we're still at a trickle pace a little bit. What would you say about earnings coming up next week? What would you point out to listeners?

BS: As I mentioned, Broadcom, I think, is the main highlight. There's a couple other, interesting ones. CrowdStrike (CRWD) is coming out that'll give some GameStop (GME) is on the calendar as well.

They just made headlines because they're buying Bitcoin with their cash reserves. So it'll be interesting to see what else they have to say about their plans for the future.

And then DocuSign (DOCU) comes out next week as well. I think that's an interesting business to business barometer to see how they're starting to pull back in the face of whatever economic challenges they might be facing.

RS: GameStop getting into Bitcoin. I'm curious to your take on that. We've seen this out of a number of stocks, some stocks a lot less attractive, let's say, to your average retail investor.

And then the Bitcoin news comes out, and they see a pop, is this about driving sentiment? Is this about gaining headline highlights that the company can get when it gets into Bitcoin? Because there's a lot of discussion around, why would you get into a stock just because they're getting into Bitcoin. If you're bullish on Bitcoin, just get into Bitcoin.

What would you say about this trend of companies getting into Bitcoin, investors getting into stocks that are getting into Bitcoin? What are your thoughts about that and how investors should be or should be encouraged to think about that?

BS: I think from a company's point of view, the goal of getting into Bitcoin (BTC-USD) is to appeal to a certain investor type.

And I think GameStop is already on the radar for that group. So it was a meme stock. We're talking about younger investors, people who are momentum-driven, people who are headline-driven.

And so I think your point about making headlines and buying into something that's already popular within that investing cohort, I think is part of it. So you can kind of think about it in terms of we were talking about how Target appeals to a certain demographic of consumer.

I think GameStop is trying to appeal to a certain demographic of investor with the move. Because like you say, I think any rational look would say, if you like GameStop buying Bitcoin, just buy Bitcoin.

There'd be no reason to have to worry about the retail part of that. If GameStop's not into Bitcoin, you think GameStop's a good company, you could buy Bitcoin and then also buy GameStop. And then your portfolio is just doing what GameStop is doing. So it feels like it's just adding volatility to the balance sheet of GameStop.

But like I said, I think it's meant to bring in a certain type of investor. I think it's meant to bring back a bit of the glory days of the meme stock era. So I think it has a different goal in mind than a strictly business one.

RS: And would you say it's also a bit of trading versus investing? The whole notion of getting in and out of those stocks, is it more of a trading game than an investment game?

BS: Yeah. I think so. I mean, the other one that jumps to mind, obviously, is Strategy (MSTR), which has huge Bitcoin holdings and moves as a proxy for Bitcoin a lot. You know, when Bitcoin moves on whatever news, then you'll see Strategy move.

And so GameStop might move more into that category depending on how aggressively they get into it. If you're a Bitcoin investor I mean, aside from the GameStop part of it, if you're a Bitcoin investor, this can only be a bullish idea.

The idea that more and more companies are going to put Bitcoin on their balance sheet. If it's becoming a proxy for cash on some company's balance sheets, then that's only more people to purchase Bitcoin. So if you're holding Bitcoin, it's certainly bullish that you have another customer out there.

So I don't know if I see it more as a step along the way of the Bitcoin journey than really a step along the way of the GameStop journey, just because the GameStop journey has been - I don't know right word - rambling in the last several years that it's hard to see where this point's due in the future.

RS: Speaking of Bitcoin, we saw a high out of them recently. What would you say what would you add to the Bitcoin conversation right now?

BS: As we're saying about, GameStop, I think the long-term play for that is still just more acceptance in more areas.

I don't think there are a lot of consumer pickups. I think anyone who owns Bitcoin as an individual is probably owning it as an investment, not as a medium of exchange in any way.

So if you're a Bitcoin bull, there's that whole market that could conceivably come into play if it starts to become more frequently used as an actual currency.

In the meantime, I think if you're a Bitcoin investor, you're thinking about getting into Bitcoin, I think that the data points you're looking for is more situations where institutional investors are picking it up, are recommending it to their clients, the more it becomes just a regular part of everyone's portfolio, that's upside for Bitcoin.

data.title: Madison Dividend Income Fund Q1 2025 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
For the first quarter, the Madison Dividend Income Fund (class Y) returned +3.2%, which outperformed the S&P 500 Index, Russell 1000 Value Index, and Lipper Equity Income peer group returns of -4.3%, +2.1% and +1.7%, respectively.

We’ve been writing for the past several quarters about the attractive investment opportunity in dividend stocks as market participants focused elsewhere on more expensive technology holdings and the excitement surrounding artificial intelligence. Our view has been that since the end of 2022, the rotation away from dividend stocks and into non-dividends paying technology stocks created a generational opportunity to own high-quality above-average dividend stocks at below-average valuations.

We maintain that view as many dividend stocks continue to trade at historically cheap valuations compared to the broad market—even after the outperformance of dividend stocks in the quarter. Our preferred valuation metric is looking at the relative yield of dividend stocks compared to the S&P 500. Relative yield is defined as a stock or portfolio’s dividend yield divided by the S&P 500 dividend yield. A high relative yield for a stock or portfolio compared to its long-term history indicates a low valuation, while a low relative yield ratio indicates a high valuation, all else equal. At the end of the first quarter, the relative yield of the Dividend Income Fund was 1.9x the S&P 500 and 1.25x the Russell 1000 Value Index, both at the high end of their historical ranges. In our view, dividend stocks continue to be on sale relative to the broad market.

We also believe the Dividend Income Fund has important defensive characteristics that may be important as the year progresses and global tariff risks increase. These favorable attributes include:

  • An absolute dividend yield of 2.6%, which compares favorably to the absolute dividend yields of 1.34% for the S&P 500 and 2.06% for the Russell 1000 Value Index.
  • A growing income stream that protects against inflation. Portfolio holdings have increased their dividends by nearly 8% on average over the past year, which is well above inflation rates over that period. Consistent annual dividend increases are a key metric we require in portfolio holdings.
  • A high-quality portfolio with strong balance sheets that could protect on the downside in a market correction. 90% of fund holdings are rated A- or better by Standard & Poor’s, which compares favorably to the S&P 500 at 33% and the Russell 1000 Value at 20%. If there is a down market and/or credit spreads begin to widen, we believe our high-quality portfolio is likely to hold up much better than the overall market.

Our Approach to Investing – Relative Yield Strategy

The Madison Dividend Income Fund’s goal is to achieve long-term outperformance over a full market cycle while taking below average risk. To pursue this objective, we employ a relative yield strategy where we buy stocks that have a dividend yield at least 1.1x the S&P 500. An attractive relative yield investment is a stock with a relative yield near the high end of its historical range and a long dividend paying history with a consistent record of dividend increases. Once we identify high relative yield stocks, we then analyze a company’s business model, balance sheet, and cash flow profile to assess its ability to continue paying dividends. We want to find stocks that have low valuations with potential for valuation multiple expansion, while avoiding stocks that may have high dividend yields but face secular challenges.

This quarter we want to highlight Honeywell (HON) as a high-quality relative yield investment in the Industrial sector. HON is a premier industrial company with leading positions in Aerospace (40% of revenues), Industrial Automation (26%), Building Automation (17%) and Safety and Productivity Solutions (17%). A common theme across these end markets is that HON embeds its products into the operations of its customers, which generates long-term recurring revenue from aftermarket products/services. We believe HON has a sustainable competitive advantage due to its scale, high customer switching costs, intangible assets, and long history of innovation. Many HON products are a low percentage of its customers’ total costs, and these products get designed into products and operations, which provide a long-term source of consistent revenue.

HON recently announced plans to break the company into three separate entities by the end of 2026, which we believe will create shareholder value. The three stand-alone companies will be Honeywell Aerospace, Honeywell Automation, and Advanced Materials. These businesses are likely to have better focus, different levels of capital intensity, and different growth profiles as stand-alone entities. Activist investor Elliott Management also owns a $5 billion stake, which is the largest activist investment the firm has made in its history. Valuation appears attractive at 18x forward expected earnings, which is a less than market multiple.

The fund originally purchased HON in September 2020 during the Covid pandemic when air travel was largely offline, which negatively impacted its flagship aerospace business. We added to the position in June 2024 as its relative yield hit the highest level since the global financial crisis in 2009, and it is now the seventh largest position in the 40-stock portfolio. HON has an A-rated balance sheet by Standard & Poor’s and has raised its dividend an average of 5% over the past five years. We expect continued dividend increases going forward.

Summary

The Madison Dividend Income Fund is a conservatively managed equity strategy that owns a high-quality portfolio of above-average dividend yield stocks with strong balance sheets and sustainable competitive advantages (wide moats). We believe that owning high-quality stocks with above-average dividend yields is the best way to provide income and generate attractive long-term returns over a full market cycle while limiting drawdowns in bear markets and market corrections.

Respectfully,

John Brown | Drew Justman

Active Share: the percentage of a portfolio that differs from its benchmark index. It can range from 0% for an index fund that perfectly mirrors its benchmark to 100% for a portfolio with no overlap with an index.

Portfolio Turnover: a measure of the trading activity in an investment portfolio—how often securities are bought and sold by a portfolio. The range represents the typical turnover of the portfolio.

Wtd Avg. Market Cap: the size of the companies in which the fund invests. Market capitalization is calculated by number of a company’s shares outstanding times its price per share.

Standard Deviation: a statistical measurement of dispersion about an average, which, for a portfolio, depicts how widely the returns varied over a certain period of time. Investors may use the standard deviation of historical performance to understand the range of returns for a portfolio. When a portfolio has a higher standard deviation than its benchmark, it implies higher relative volatility. Standard deviation has been calculated

using the trailing monthly total returns for the appropriate time period. The standard deviation values are annualized.

Upside Capture Ratio: a fund’s performance in up markets relative to its benchmark. The security’s upside capture return is divided by the benchmark’s upside capture return over the time period.

Downside Capture Ratio: a fund’s performance in down markets relative to its benchmark. The security’s downside capture return is divided by the benchmark’s downside capture return over the time period.

Beta: a measure of the fund’s sensitivity to market movements. A portfolio with a beta greater than 1 is more volatile than the market, and a portfolio with a beta less than 1 is less volatile than the market.

data.title: Yet Another Wrinkle In The Trade Policy Muddle
data.description:
So much for getting clarity on President Trump’s import tariff policy by his July 9 deadline for trade deals to be struck with countries wanting to avoid reciprocal import tariffs. On Thursday, the Court for International Trade struck down key parts of the Trump import tariff policy.

That now adds to the difficulty of providing investors and markets with the trade policy certainty for which they have been clamoring. This does not bode well for the economic outlook or for the stock market’s longer-run performance.

It would be an understatement to say that Trump’s import tariff policy has engendered a considerable amount of economic uncertainty and market volatility. With alarming frequency, large tariff increases have been announced only to be paused for trade deals to be worked out.

One example of this was the “liberation day” announcement on April 2 of punitive reciprocal import tariffs to be applied to about 60 countries by April 9. That was to be over and above a 10 percent baseline-tariff to be applied to all countries.

However, buckling to a sharp adverse reaction in both the stock market and the US government bond market, Trump announced a 90-day pause in the application of those tariffs. That pause was supposed to allow time for favorable trade deals to be negotiated that would absolve those countries from the threatened reciprocal tariffs.

It would also be an understatement to say that progress in negotiating trade deals has been disappointingly slow. To date, more than halfway through the 90-day pause period, a partial framework for a trade deal has been negotiated with the United Kingdom.

In addition, a 90-day truce in the US-China trade war was negotiated in Geneva at the same time that the US reduced its tariff rate on China from 145 percent to 40 percent. This leaves around 60 trade deals yet to be negotiated in over a little more than a month.

Now the US Court for International Trade has added an additional layer of tariff policy uncertainty. It has done so by ruling that Trump had exceeded his authority in imposing his “liberation day” tariffs. The court found that those tariffs were unconstitutional on which basis it permanently enjoined the administration from collecting those tariffs.

To be sure, Trump will appeal that decision in the hope that in the end the Supreme Court will rule in his favor. However, this could prove to be a lengthy process. It could also incentivize countries to go slow in their trade negotiations with the United States until they had greater clarity on where US tariff policy stood. They would not want to make unilateral concessions to Trump now if there was a reasonable chance that he will not get his way with the courts.

If the Supreme Court strikes down Trump’s request for an over-ruling of the Court for International Trade’s decision, it would help the country avoid a tariff-induced spike in consumer prices and the risk of an economic recession.

However, judging by the Supreme Court’s composition, it is likely that Trump’s appeal will be upheld. Needless to add, one can be sure that Trump will reject the gift horse that the Court for International Trade is offering him by giving him an off-ramp from his economically destructive trade policy.

The bottom line, is that markets will now have to deal with an additional layer of trade policy uncertainty caused by the Court for International Trade’s decision.

Even if that uncertainty is soon eliminated by the Supreme Court, markets will still be faced with the uncertainty as to how many trade deals will be stuck by the July 9 deadline and what happens on July 9 if deals are not struck. The markets will also have to grapple with how the trade war between the United States and China, the world’s two largest economies, will be resolved.
